title: Pediatric peripheral IV insertion
executiveSummary: Insert a pediatric peripheral IV catheter using an aseptic non-touch technique, selecting the gauge by weight, ensuring appropriate analgesia and safe therapeutic restraint, and monitoring closely for infiltration and extravasation.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Identify the patient (two identifiers), check allergies, and verify the prescription; select the catheter gauge and anatomical site based on weight.
2. Explain the procedure to the family and the child; provide topical or systemic analgesia and arrange safe therapeutic restraint (minimum two people).
3. Perform hand hygiene (WHO Moment 2) and prepare sterile materials.
4. Position the extremity; apply a gentle tourniquet 5–10 cm proximal to the site; palpate and select the vein.
5. Disinfect the skin with an antiseptic using circular friction; allow to dry completely; do not re-touch the disinfected area.
6. Perform venipuncture using an aseptic non-touch technique (10–30° angle); advance until blood flashback is seen in the chamber.
7. Remove the tourniquet; advance the catheter into the vein, withdraw the needle, and connect a closed needleless system without unnecessary disconnections.
8. Verify blood return and patency by infusing fluid without resistance; secure with a transparent dressing without causing compression.
9. Document date, time, site, gauge, number of attempts, and operator; perform hand hygiene upon completion.
10. If unsuccessful: allow a maximum of two attempts per operator, then escalate to an experienced colleague or consider alternative access (intraosseous, central).
11. Assess the site every shift for erythema, pain, edema, and extravasation; disinfect the hub before every access; remove the device when treatment is completed or if a complication arises.

## Scientific rationale

### Indications

- Administration of IV medications or fluids.
- Fluid resuscitation with boluses (dehydration, sepsis, shock).
- Blood sampling when alternative access is unavailable.
- Contrast media administration or procedures per medical order.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **Prevention bundle (WHO 2024 / SATI):** Hand hygiene, aseptic technique, skin antisepsis, a maximum of two attempts per operator, adequate securement, and early removal when access is no longer essential (Gorski et al., 2024; CDC/HICPAC, 2011).

### Site and gauge selection

| Approximate weight | Suggested gauge | Preferred site |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Newborn / small infant | 24 G | Dorsum of hand or foot; forearm if vein is visible |
| 1–10 kg | 24 G | Forearm, dorsum of hand |
| 10–20 kg | 22–24 G | Forearm |
| > 20 kg | 22–20 G | Forearm (first choice) |

| Preference | Site |
| --- | --- |
| First choice | Forearm (basilic, cephalic, or median cubital veins) |
| Alternative | Dorsum of the hand or foot (infants; higher extravasation risk) |
| Last resort | Scalp (newborns/infants only, per institutional protocol) |
| Avoid | Extremities with infection, lymphedema, fistula, or prior failed punctures in the same area |

Rotate extremities; do not repeat a puncture in the same site during the same session.

### Preparing the child

- Topical analgesia when time permits (EMLA 60 minutes, topical lidocaine, J-tip per AAP protocol).
- Therapeutic restraint: one person stabilizes the child without compressing the chest or airway; another operator performs cannulation.
- Antiseptic: use alcoholic chlorhexidine in children ≥2 months of age; in premature newborns or infants <2 months, follow institutional protocols.

### Criteria for immediate removal

- Phlebitis, infiltration, or extravasation (activate the specific protocol if a vesicant drug is involved).
- Suspicion of local site infection or catheter-related bloodstream infection.
- Malfunction or unresolved obstruction.
- Catheter is no longer essential.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Extravasation of vesicant, hyperosmolar, or vasoactive medications.
- Extensive hematoma, persistent bleeding, or loss of distal pulse.
- Cyanosis or pallor of the cannulated extremity.
- Severe pain, inconsolable crying, or refusal to use the extremity.
- Fever or progressive erythema at the insertion site.

bibliography.citation: World Health Organization. Guidelines for the prevention of bloodstream infections and other infections associated with the use of intravascular catheters — Part 1: Peripheral catheters. WHO, 2024.
OMS — Noticias: nuevas orientaciones para reducir septicemias por catéteres (2024).
Gorski LA, Hadaway L, Hagle ME, et al. Infusion Therapy Standards of Practice, 9th ed. J Infus Nurs. 2024.
O'Grady NP, Alexander M, Burns LA, et al. Guidelines for the Prevention of Intravascular Catheter-Related Infections, 2011. CDC / HICPAC.
American Academy of Pediatrics. Pediatric Pain Management and sedation for procedures.
SATI — Comité de Infectología Crítica. Prevención de IAAS y accesos vasculares.
